# X-ray Absorbance Spectroscopy (XAS)

![X-ray Absorbance Spectroscopy Main Image](https://covalentmetrology.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/10/EXAFS-chi-k.png)

X-ray absorption spectroscopy is a technique used to analyze the electronic structure of atoms and molecules by measuring the energy absorption of X-rays as they interact with a sample, providing insights into its chemical composition and bonding. 

### Strengths

### Limitations

### Learn More

## X-ray Absorbance Spectroscopy Services

### X-ray Absorbance Spectroscopy

![Example Output 6286](https://covalentmetrology.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/10/EXAFS-chi-k.png)

#### X-ray Absorbance Spectroscopy

X-ray absorption spectroscopy is a technique used to analyze the electronic structure of atoms and molecules by measuring the energy absorption of X-rays as they interact with a sample, providing insights into its chemical composition and bonding. 

### X-ray Absorption Near Edge Spectroscopy

![Example Output 6286-1](https://covalentmetrology.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/10/XANES.png)

#### X-ray Absorption Near Edge Spectroscopy

X-ray absorption near edge spectroscopy (XANES) is a technique that analyzes the fine structure of X-ray absorption spectra near the absorption edges of specific elements in a sample, providing detailed information about their chemical state and local atomic environment.

**Strengths**

### Extended X-ray Absorption Fine Structure

![Example Output 6286-2](https://covalentmetrology.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/10/exafs.png)

#### Extended X-ray Absorption Fine Structure

Extended X-ray absorption fine structure (EXAFS) is a spectroscopic technique that analyzes the oscillations in the X-ray absorption spectrum beyond the absorption edge to provide information about the local atomic structure and coordination environment of specific elements within a sample.

**Strengths**

**Limitations**

### X-ray Emission Spectroscopy

![Example Output 6286-3](https://covalentmetrology.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/10/FeKbeta_XES_white.webp)

#### X-ray Emission Spectroscopy

X-ray emission spectroscopy is a technique that analyzes the energy and intensity of X-rays emitted by a sample after it has been irradiated with X-rays providing information about the electronic structure and composition of the material.

**Strengths**

Example plot from: [*easyXAFS.com*](https://www.easyxafs.com/instrumentdata)  
*Fe Kβ spectra showing sensitivity to changes in oxidation state and local coordination environment for reference standards: Fe and Fe (III) [Fe2O3].*

### X-ray Pair Distribution Function

![Example Output 6286-4](https://covalentmetrology.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/10/Pair-distribution-functions-for-pristine-as-deposited-room-temperature-annealed.png)

#### X-ray Pair Distribution Function

X-ray pair distribution function (PDF) analysis is a technique used to determine the three-dimensional atomic or molecular structure of amorphous or crystalline materials from their X-ray scattering patterns, providing insights into the arrangement of atoms in a sample without the need for long-range order.

**Strengths**

**Limitations**

Example plot from: *Shyam, Badri & Stone, Kevin & Bassiri, Riccardo & Fejer, Martin & Toney, Michael & Mehta, Apurva. (2016). Measurement and Modeling of Short and Medium Range Order in Amorphous Ta2O5 Thin Films. Scientific Reports. 6. 32170. 10.1038/srep32170.*

## Contents

# X-ray absorption spectroscopy

|  |  |

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/9/99/Question_book-new.svg/60px-Question_book-new.svg.png)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/XASEdges.svg/250px-XASEdges.svg.png)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/XASFig.jpg/250px-XASFig.jpg)

**X-ray [absorption spectroscopy](/wiki/Absorption_spectroscopy "Absorption spectroscopy")** (XAS) is a set of advanced techniques used for probing the local environment of matter at atomic level and its electronic structure.[[1]](#cite_note-1) The experiments require access to [synchrotron radiation](/wiki/Synchrotron_radiation "Synchrotron radiation") facilities for their intense and tunable [X-ray](/wiki/X-ray "X-ray") beams. Samples can be in the gas phase, solutions, or solids.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Background

XAS data are obtained by tuning the [photon](/wiki/Photon "Photon") energy,[[3]](#cite_note-3) using a crystalline monochromator, to a range where [core electrons](/wiki/Core_electron "Core electron") can be excited (0.1-100 keV). The edges are, in part, named by which core electron is excited: the [principal quantum numbers](/wiki/Principal_quantum_number "Principal quantum number") n = 1, 2, and 3, correspond to the K-, L-, and M-edges, respectively.[[4]](#cite_note-4) For instance, excitation of a 1s electron occurs at the [K-edge](/wiki/Metal_K-edge "Metal K-edge"), while excitation of a 2s or 2p electron occurs at an [L-edge](/wiki/Metal_L-edge "Metal L-edge") (Figure 1).

There are three main regions found on a spectrum generated by XAS data, which are then thought of as separate spectroscopic techniques (Figure 2):

XAS is a type of [absorption spectroscopy](/wiki/Absorption_spectroscopy "Absorption spectroscopy") from a core initial state with a well-defined symmetry; therefore, the quantum mechanical [selection rules](/wiki/Selection_rules "Selection rules") select the symmetry of the final states in the continuum, which are usually a mixture of multiple components. The most intense features are due to electric-dipole allowed transitions (i.e. *Δℓ* = ± 1) to unoccupied final states. For example, the most intense features of a K-edge are due to core transitions from 1s → p-like final states, while the most intense features of the L3-edge are due to 2p → d-like final states.

XAS methodology can be broadly divided into four experimental categories that can yield complementary results: [metal K-edge](/wiki/Metal_K-edge "Metal K-edge"), [metal L-edge](/wiki/Metal_L-edge "Metal L-edge"), [ligand K-edge](/wiki/Ligand_K-edge "Ligand K-edge"), and EXAFS.

The most obvious means of mapping heterogeneous samples beyond x-ray absorption contrast is through elemental analysis by x-ray fluorescence, similar to EDX methods in electron microscopy.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Applications

XAS is a technique used in various scientific fields, including [molecular](/wiki/Molecular_physics "Molecular physics") and [condensed matter physics](/wiki/Condensed_matter_physics "Condensed matter physics"),[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) [materials science and engineering](/wiki/Materials_science_and_engineering "Materials science and engineering"), [chemistry](/wiki/Chemistry "Chemistry"), [earth science](/wiki/Earth_science "Earth science"), and [biology](/wiki/Biology "Biology"). In particular, its unique sensitivity to the local structure, as compared to [x-ray diffraction](/wiki/X-ray_diffraction "X-ray diffraction"), has been exploited for studying:

enriched_points.expanded_content: X-ray Absorption Spectroscopy (XAS) investigates the electronic structure of atoms by measuring X-ray energy absorption, revealing insights into chemical bonding and electronic states.
XAS effectively determines oxidation states of elements, crucial for understanding redox processes in materials, particularly in catalysis and energy storage applications.
XAS provides detailed information about the local atomic environment surrounding specific elements, enabling insights into coordination numbers and bond lengths.
XAS is widely used in catalysis research to study active sites and mechanisms, enhancing the design of more efficient catalysts for chemical reactions.
Combining XAS with techniques like X-ray diffraction and electron microscopy enhances structural and electronic characterization, providing a comprehensive understanding of materials.
